name: Acne Vulgaris
description: Common skin condition causing pimples, blackheads, and inflamed lesions due to clogged hair follicles.
causes: Acne is a disorder of the pilosebaceous unit influenced by androgens. The precursor lesion is the microcomedone which develops into non-inflammatory lesions (comedones) and inflammatory lesions (papules and pustules).
treatment: Benzoyl peroxide, salicylic acid, retinoids, antibiotics for severe cases
risk_factors: Teenagers, young adults, hormonal fluctuations
when_to_see_doctor: If OTC treatments ineffective for 2-3 months. If severe cysts or nodules develop. If scarring occurs. If causing psychological distress.
seasonality: Typically begins in adolescence (10-16 years). In girls may start as early as 10. Peaks at 13-16. Premenstrual exacerbation common in women. Late-onset acne (20s) more common in women.
common_in: Teenagers, young adults, hormonal fluctuations
prevention: Avoid squeezing comedones. Cleanse with gentle cleanser. Use non-comedogenic moisturizers. Benzoyl peroxide can prevent new lesions.

name: Rosacea
description: Chronic facial redness with visible blood vessels, flushing, and sometimes acne-like bumps.
treatment: Topical metronidazole, azelaic acid, laser therapy, sun protection
risk_factors: Fair-skinned adults 30-50, family history
when_to_see_doctor: If persistent facial redness. If eye symptoms (dryness, irritation, swelling). If bumps are painful.
seasonality: Often worsens with sun exposure, hot weather, spicy foods, and alcohol.
common_in: Fair-skinned adults 30-50, family history
prevention: SPF 50+ daily sunscreen. Avoid triggers: spicy foods, alcohol, extreme temperatures. Gentle skincare routine.

name: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
description: Chronic inflammatory skin condition causing dry, itchy, and red patches, often with an allergic basis.
causes: Atopy means an inherited predisposition to eczema, asthma or hay fever. Atopic individuals may have one or all of these conditions.
treatment: Moisturizers, topical corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, avoiding triggers
risk_factors: Children, family history of allergies or asthma
when_to_see_doctor: If severe itching disturbs sleep. If signs of infection (weeping, crusting, fever). If condition significantly impacts quality of life.
seasonality: Usually begins between 3-12 months. Long-term condition that clears by puberty in 90% of individuals.
common_in: Children, family history of allergies or asthma
prevention: Complete emollient therapy: soap substitutes, bath oil, moisturizer. Cotton clothing. Avoid irritants. Maintain cool, humidified environment.

name: Psoriasis
description: Autoimmune condition causing rapid skin cell buildup with thick, silvery-scaled plaques.
causes: Psoriasis is a common disease affecting about 3% of the population. It is linked to several genes with familial occurrence. It may be precipitated by hormonal changes, streptococcal throat infection, trauma, medications, and emotional stress.
treatment: Topical steroids, vitamin D analogs, phototherapy, biologics for severe cases
risk_factors: Adults 15-35, family history, triggered by stress or infection
when_to_see_doctor: If more than 30% of body surface affected. If joint pain develops (possible psoriatic arthritis). If pustular or erythrodermic forms appear (EMERGENCY).
seasonality: Often begins between 15-25 years. Can occur at any age. Typically waxes and wanes with periods of relapse and remission.
common_in: Adults 15-35, family history, triggered by stress or infection
prevention: Avoid known triggers: stress, infections, skin injuries, certain medications. Moderate sun exposure. Maintain healthy weight.

name: Contact Dermatitis
description: Allergic or irritant reaction to substances touching the skin.
treatment: Avoid allergen, topical corticosteroids, cold compresses, antihistamines
risk_factors: Can affect anyone, common with jewelry, cosmetics, plants
when_to_see_doctor: If severe blistering. If cause cannot be identified. If infection suspected. If facial or genital involvement.
seasonality: Depends on allergen/irritant exposure, not seasonal per se.
common_in: Can affect anyone, common with jewelry, cosmetics, plants
prevention: Identify and avoid triggering substances. Use barrier creams. Patch testing to identify allergens.

name: Melasma
description: Brown or gray-brown patches on the face, triggered by sun exposure and hormonal changes.
treatment: Sun protection, hydroquinone, azelaic acid, chemical peels, laser
risk_factors: Women during pregnancy, hormonal therapy, darker skin types
when_to_see_doctor: If pigmentation persists despite sun protection. If affecting quality of life.
seasonality: Worsens with sun exposure. More noticeable in summer. Common during pregnancy and hormonal therapy.
common_in: Women during pregnancy, hormonal therapy, darker skin types
prevention: Strict sun protection: SPF 50+ daily, wide-brimmed hats, avoid peak sun hours. Gentle skincare without irritation.

name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
description: Scaly, oily patches on the scalp, face, and other sebum-rich areas.
treatment: Antifungal shampoos, topical corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors
risk_factors: Adults 30-60, infants (cradle cap)
when_to_see_doctor: If over-the-counter treatments are ineffective after 4 weeks. If severe itching or inflammation. If hair loss occurs.
seasonality: Often worsens in winter/dry conditions. May improve in summer with UV exposure.
common_in: Adults 30-60, infants (cradle cap)
prevention: Regular washing with medicated shampoos containing zinc pyrithione, ketoconazole, or selenium sulfide. Manage stress.

name: Urticaria (Hives)
description: Raised, itchy welts caused by histamine release.
causes: Caused by release of histamine and other chemicals from mast cells in the skin. This causes small blood vessels to leak, resulting in tissue swelling (weals).
treatment: Antihistamines, avoid triggers, corticosteroids for severe episodes
risk_factors: All ages, foods, medications, stress, heat
when_to_see_doctor: EMERGENCY: If accompanied by difficulty breathing, throat/tongue swelling. If chronic (lasting >6 weeks). If unknown cause.
seasonality: Affects both adults and children.
common_in: All ages, foods, medications, stress, heat
prevention: Identify and avoid triggers (foods, medications, physical stimuli). Antihistamines for prevention if chronic.

name: Tinea (Ringworm, Athlete's Foot)
description: Fungal skin infection causing ring-shaped, scaly patches.
treatment: Topical antifungals (clotrimazole, terbinafine), oral antifungals
risk_factors: Warm humid climates, athletes, communal showers, pets
when_to_see_doctor: If OTC antifungals fail after 2 weeks. If nails are involved. If condition spreads rapidly.
seasonality: More common in warm, humid months. Athlete's foot prevalent in summer.
common_in: Warm humid climates, athletes, communal showers, pets
prevention: Keep feet dry. Wear flip-flops in communal showers. Avoid sharing towels. Treat pets if source of infection.

name: Herpes Simplex (Cold Sores)
description: Viral infection causing small, painful blisters.
treatment: Antiviral creams (acyclovir), oral antivirals for outbreaks
risk_factors: Most adults carry HSV-1, transmitted through close contact
when_to_see_doctor: If outbreaks are frequent (>6/year) or severe. If lesions don't heal within 2 weeks. If immunocompromised.
seasonality: Triggers include stress, illness, fever, sun exposure, and cold weather.
common_in: Most adults carry HSV-1, transmitted through close contact
prevention: Avoid triggers. Sunscreen on lips. Lysine supplements may help some. Antiviral medication for frequent outbreaks.

name: Actinic Keratosis
description: Rough, scaly precancerous patches on sun-damaged skin.
treatment: Cryotherapy, topical chemotherapy (5-FU), photodynamic therapy
risk_factors: Fair-skinned adults over 40, chronic sun exposure
when_to_see_doctor: ALL cases should be evaluated. If lesions become tender, bleed, or grow rapidly.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Fair-skinned adults over 40, chronic sun exposure
prevention: Strict sun protection. Regular skin checks. Treat promptly to prevent progression to SCC.

name: Basal Cell Carcinoma
description: Most common skin cancer, slowly growing on sun-exposed areas.
causes: Due to cumulative sun damage. Occurs in fair-skinned people who have worked or had hobbies outdoors. Most occur on the face. Metastatic spread is rare.
treatment: Surgical excision, Mohs surgery, cryotherapy, topical imiquimod
risk_factors: Fair-skinned adults over 50, chronic sun exposure
when_to_see_doctor: ANY suspicious lesion needs dermatology evaluation. If sore doesn't heal within 3-4 weeks.
seasonality: Most common in middle age or older.
common_in: Fair-skinned adults over 50, chronic sun exposure
prevention: Sun protection from childhood. Regular skin examinations. Avoid tanning beds.

name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma
description: Second most common skin cancer, can spread if untreated.
causes: Invasive carcinoma arising from sun-damaged skin. Can arise from pre-existing lesions like Bowen's disease or actinic keratosis. Common on sun-exposed sites.
treatment: Surgical excision, Mohs surgery, radiation, chemotherapy
risk_factors: Fair-skinned adults over 50, chronic sun exposure
when_to_see_doctor: URGENT: Any firm, growing nodule or non-healing sore on sun-exposed skin.
seasonality: Most common in middle age or older.
common_in: Fair-skinned adults over 50, chronic sun exposure
prevention: Sun protection. Regular dermatology screening. Monitor sun-damaged skin.

name: Malignant Melanoma
description: Most dangerous skin cancer, requires urgent treatment.
causes: Malignant tumour of pigment-producing cells (melanocytes). Two-thirds arise from normal skin, one-third from pre-existing moles. Can metastasise through lymphatic and circulatory systems.
treatment: Surgical excision, sentinel lymph node biopsy, immunotherapy
risk_factors: Fair skin, severe sunburns, many moles, family history
when_to_see_doctor: EMERGENCY: Use ABCDE rule (Asymmetry, Border irregular, Color variation, Diameter >6mm, Evolving). Early detection saves lives.
seasonality: All age groups affected. Those with fair/red hair who burn rather than tan are at highest risk.
common_in: Fair skin, severe sunburns, many moles, family history
prevention: Sun protection from childhood. Regular skin checks. Monthly self-exams using ABCDE rule. Avoid tanning beds.

name: Vitiligo
description: Autoimmune condition causing white patches due to loss of melanocytes.
treatment: Topical corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, phototherapy, skin grafting
risk_factors: All skin types, often before age 20, family history
when_to_see_doctor: If depigmentation spreads rapidly. If affecting quality of life. To discuss treatment options.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: All skin types, often before age 20, family history
prevention: Sun protection on depigmented areas (they sunburn easily). Cosmetic camouflage. Avoid triggers if identifiable.

name: Alopecia Areata
description: Autoimmune condition causing patchy hair loss.
symptoms: Can progress to total hair loss
treatment: Topical minoxidil, corticosteroids, JAK inhibitors
risk_factors: All ages, family history of autoimmune conditions
when_to_see_doctor: If hair loss is rapid or extensive. If affecting eyebrows/eyelashes. For treatment options including JAK inhibitors.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: All ages, family history of autoimmune conditions
prevention: No proven prevention. Manage stress. Early treatment may help limit hair loss extent.

name: Keratosis Pilaris
description: Harmless condition causing small, rough bumps on skin.
treatment: Moisturizers with urea or lactic acid, gentle exfoliation, topical retinoids
risk_factors: Children and adolescents, often with eczema or dry skin
when_to_see_doctor: Not medically necessary. If skin feels rough or appearance is bothersome despite moisturizing.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Children and adolescents, often with eczema or dry skin
prevention: Regular moisturizing with urea or lactic acid. Gentle exfoliation. Humidifier in dry environments.

name: Impetigo
description: Contagious bacterial skin infection common in children.
causes: Superficial infection of the epidermis caused by Staphylococcus aureus or group A beta-haemolytic streptococcus. Entrance gained through broken skin.
treatment: Topical antibiotics (mupirocin), oral antibiotics for widespread infection
risk_factors: Children 2-5, poor hygiene, warm climates
when_to_see_doctor: If widespread. If fever develops. If not improving after 48 hours of OTC treatment. Prompt treatment prevents spread.
seasonality: Common in childhood. Highly contagious.
common_in: Children 2-5, poor hygiene, warm climates
prevention: Good hygiene: regular handwashing, avoid sharing towels and facecloths, keep nails short.

name: Cellulitis
description: Deep bacterial skin infection requiring prompt treatment.
causes: Infection of subcutaneous tissues caused by group A, C or haemolytic streptococcus. Usually follows a portal of entry such as leg ulcer, tinea pedis, eczema, or insect bite.
treatment: Oral or IV antibiotics (cephalexin, clindamycin), elevation, pain management
risk_factors: Adults, broken skin, diabetes, immunocompromised, obesity
when_to_see_doctor: EMERGENCY: Seek immediate care for red, swollen, warm skin. Especially with fever, chills, or red streaks.
seasonality: More common in older people but can occur at any age.
common_in: Adults, broken skin, diabetes, immunocompromised, obesity
prevention: Keep skin clean and moisturized. Promptly treat cuts, scrapes, and fungal infections like athlete's foot. Manage diabetes if present.

name: Shingles (Herpes Zoster)
description: Painful blistering rash from chickenpox virus reactivation.
causes: Reactivation of varicella zoster virus (chickenpox) lying dormant in the dorsal root ganglion. The virus travels down cutaneous nerves to infect epidermal cells.
treatment: Antivirals (acyclovir, valacyclovir), pain management, vaccines
risk_factors: Adults over 50, immunocompromised, prior chickenpox
when_to_see_doctor: Within 48 hours of rash for antivirals. EMERGENCY if eye involvement. If severe pain. If immunocompromised.
seasonality: Can occur at any age. Post-herpetic neuralgia may persist for months or years in older people.
common_in: Adults over 50, immunocompromised, prior chickenpox
prevention: Vaccination (Shingrix) recommended for adults 50+ and immunocompromised. Healthy lifestyle to maintain immune function.

name: Molluscum Contagiosum
description: Viral skin infection causing dome-shaped bumps.
treatment: Often self-resolves, cryotherapy, curettage, topical cantharidin
risk_factors: Children 1-10, sexually active adults, immunocompromised
when_to_see_doctor: If widespread (>20 lesions). If on face or genitals. If immunocompromised. If lesions become red and painful.
seasonality: More common in warm, humid conditions. Spreads in schools and swimming pools.
common_in: Children 1-10, sexually active adults, immunocompromised
prevention: Good hygiene. Avoid sharing towels. Do not scratch lesions. Keep lesions covered.

name: Lichen Planus
description: Inflammatory condition causing itchy, purple bumps.
treatment: Topical corticosteroids, antihistamines, phototherapy, oral retinoids
risk_factors: Adults 30-60, associated with hepatitis C, stress triggers
when_to_see_doctor: If oral lesions cause burning pain. If widespread. If nail destruction occurs. To differentiate from other conditions.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Adults 30-60, associated with hepatitis C, stress triggers
prevention: Avoid known triggers. Manage stress. Good oral hygiene. Avoid tobacco and alcohol if oral lesions present.

name: Granuloma Annulare
description: Benign condition causing ring-shaped bumps.
treatment: Often self-resolves, topical corticosteroids, cryotherapy
risk_factors: Children and young adults, more common in women
when_to_see_doctor: Usually benign. If widespread form appears. If diagnosis uncertain.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Children and young adults, more common in women
prevention: No known prevention. Condition is benign and often self-resolving.

name: Pityriasis Rosea
description: Self-limiting rash with herald patch.
treatment: Self-resolving, antihistamines for itch, topical corticosteroids
risk_factors: Young adults 10-35, more common in spring/fall
when_to_see_doctor: If symptoms persist despite home care, worsen significantly, or if diagnosis is uncertain.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Young adults 10-35, more common in spring/fall
prevention: Maintain good skin hygiene. Moisturize regularly. Protect skin from excessive sun exposure.

name: Dyshidrotic Eczema
description: Type of eczema with small, itchy blisters on hands and feet.
treatment: Topical corticosteroids, soaking & drying, avoiding triggers
risk_factors: Adults 20-40, stress, warm weather, nickel sensitivity
when_to_see_doctor: If unable to use hands normally. If secondary infection suspected. If persistent despite treatment.
seasonality: More common in warm weather. Stress triggers. Associated with nickel sensitivity.
common_in: Adults 20-40, stress, warm weather, nickel sensitivity
prevention: Avoid triggers: stress, nickel, cobalt. Use gentle cleansers. Keep hands dry. Identify contact allergens.

name: Hidradenitis Suppurativa
description: Chronic condition causing painful lumps in skin folds.
treatment: Antibiotics, anti-inflammatories, biologics, laser, surgery
risk_factors: Young adults, smokers, obesity, family history
when_to_see_doctor: If recurrent painful lumps. Early referral to dermatology improves outcomes. If draining sinus tracts form.
seasonality: Flares more common in summer due to heat and sweating.
common_in: Young adults, smokers, obesity, family history
prevention: Weight management. Smoking cessation. Loose-fitting clothing. Gentle cleansing with antiseptic washes.

name: Erythema Nodosum
description: Inflammatory condition causing painful red nodules on shins.
treatment: Treat underlying cause, NSAIDs, rest, potassium iodide
risk_factors: Young women, infections, sarcoidosis, IBD
when_to_see_doctor: To identify underlying cause. If fever, joint pain, or gastrointestinal symptoms present. If lesions ulcerate.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Young women, infections, sarcoidosis, IBD
prevention: Treat underlying cause. Leg elevation. Support stockings. Bed rest during acute phase.

name: Xerosis (Dry Skin)
description: Extremely dry skin from environmental factors or aging.
treatment: Regular moisturizing, gentle cleansers, humidifiers, avoid hot showers
risk_factors: Elderly, dry climates, frequent bathing, diabetes
when_to_see_doctor: If severe itching interferes with sleep. If skin cracks or bleeds. If signs of infection.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Elderly, dry climates, frequent bathing, diabetes
prevention: Regular moisturizing. Gentle cleansers. Humidifier in dry climates. Avoid hot showers. Drink adequate water.

name: Sunburn
description: Acute skin damage from UV radiation.
treatment: Cool compresses, aloe vera, moisturizers, NSAIDs, hydration
risk_factors: Anyone with sun exposure, fair skin more susceptible
when_to_see_doctor: If severe blistering over large area. If fever, chills, nausea. If signs of heat exhaustion or heatstroke.
seasonality: More common in summer and peak UV hours (10am-4pm). Risk increases at higher altitudes and near reflective surfaces.
common_in: Anyone with sun exposure, fair skin more susceptible
prevention: SPF 30+ broad spectrum sunscreen. Reapply every 2 hours. Seek shade 10am-4pm. Protective clothing and hats.

name: Stretch Marks (Striae)
description: Linear scars from rapid skin stretching.
treatment: Topical retinoids, laser therapy, microneedling, hyaluronic acid
risk_factors: Pregnancy, puberty, rapid weight gain, corticosteroid use
when_to_see_doctor: Not medically necessary. If rapid onset with other symptoms (Cushing's syndrome). For cosmetic concerns.
seasonality: Not seasonally dependent. Related to rapid growth, pregnancy, or weight gain.
common_in: Pregnancy, puberty, rapid weight gain, corticosteroid use
prevention: Maintain stable weight. Keep skin well-hydrated. Gentle exfoliation. Moisturize during pregnancy.

name: Hyperhidrosis
description: Excessive sweating beyond normal thermoregulation.
treatment: Antiperspirants, iontophoresis, botulinum toxin, surgery
risk_factors: Young adults, family history, anxiety, menopause, thyroid
when_to_see_doctor: If excessive sweating interferes with daily activities. If social anxiety develops. If accompanied by weight loss or fever.
seasonality: Often worse in warm weather and during anxiety/stress.
common_in: Young adults, family history, anxiety, menopause, thyroid
prevention: Clinical strength antiperspirants. Avoid triggers (spicy foods, caffeine). Wear breathable fabrics.

name: Folliculitis
description: Inflammation of hair follicles.
treatment: Topical antibiotics (clindamycin), antifungal washes, warm compresses
risk_factors: Hot tubs, shaving, tight clothing, diabetes
when_to_see_doctor: If condition spreads or recurs frequently. If painful or with fever. If hot tub use and pseudomonas suspected.
seasonality: More common in hot, humid weather. Can occur after shaving or hot tub use.
common_in: Hot tubs, shaving, tight clothing, diabetes
prevention: Shave in direction of hair growth. Use clean razors. Avoid tight clothing. Avoid hot tubs if prone.

name: Sebaceous Cyst
description: Benign lump under skin filled with keratin.
treatment: Often left alone, surgical excision for cosmetic reasons or infection
risk_factors: Adults, acne-prone skin, genetic predisposition
when_to_see_doctor: If becomes red, painful, or inflamed (infected). If rapidly growing. If in a cosmetically concerning area.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Adults, acne-prone skin, genetic predisposition
prevention: Not preventable. Do not squeeze. Seek removal if inflamed or for cosmetic reasons.

name: Dermatofibroma
description: Benign skin growth of fibrous tissue.
treatment: Benign, can be left alone, surgical excision if symptomatic
risk_factors: Adults, more common in women, after minor injury
when_to_see_doctor: Not medically necessary. If changes in color, size, or becomes painful.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Adults, more common in women, after minor injury
prevention: No prevention needed. Benign condition.

name: Skin Tag (Acrochordon)
description: Small, soft, flesh-colored growths on a stalk.
treatment: Benign, can be removed by snip excision, cryotherapy, or cautery
risk_factors: Adults over 40, obesity, diabetes, pregnancy
when_to_see_doctor: Not medically necessary. If becomes irritated or bleeds. For cosmetic removal.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Adults over 40, obesity, diabetes, pregnancy
prevention: Weight management. Avoid friction in skin folds. Not preventable in most cases.

name: Chickenpox (Varicella)
description: Highly contagious viral infection causing itchy blisters.
causes: Varicella zoster virus. Commonly occurs in childhood. By age 10 most children in urban communities have been infected. Primary infection confers long-term immunity.
treatment: Antivirals (acyclovir), calamine lotion, antihistamines, vaccination
risk_factors: Children, unvaccinated individuals
when_to_see_doctor: If adult with chickenpox (more severe). If immunocompromised. If difficulty breathing. If signs of bacterial superinfection.
seasonality: Incubation period 13-17 days. Communicable from 5 days before rash until 6 days after.
common_in: Children, unvaccinated individuals
prevention: Vaccination (Varicella vaccine) is highly effective and recommended for children.

name: Warts (Verruca Vulgaris)
description: Benign skin growths caused by HPV.
treatment: Salicylic acid, cryotherapy, cantharidin, laser, immunotherapy
risk_factors: Children, young adults, immunocompromised
when_to_see_doctor: If painful. If spreading rapidly. If home treatments fail. If on face or genitals.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Children, young adults, immunocompromised
prevention: Avoid touching warts on others. Keep feet covered in communal areas. Do not bite nails or pick at warts.

name: Candidiasis (Yeast Infection)
description: Fungal infection in moist skin folds.
treatment: Topical antifungals (clotrimazole, nystatin), oral fluconazole
risk_factors: Infants, diabetics, antibiotic use, immunocompromised
when_to_see_doctor: If severe or recurrent. If diabetic. If immunocompromised. If OTC treatments fail.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Infants, diabetics, antibiotic use, immunocompromised
prevention: Keep skin dry. Wear breathable fabrics. Manage diabetes. Avoid unnecessary antibiotics.

name: Lupus Erythematosus
description: Autoimmune disease with skin involvement.
treatment: Sun protection, antimalarials (hydroxychloroquine), corticosteroids
risk_factors: Women 15-45, African American & Hispanic, family history
when_to_see_doctor: If butterfly rash or photosensitivity. If fatigue, joint pain, fever. EMERGENCY if kidney involvement suspected.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Women 15-45, African American & Hispanic, family history
prevention: Strict sun protection. Avoid UV light. Healthy lifestyle. Early treatment of flares.

name: Scleroderma
description: Autoimmune condition causing hardening and tightening of skin.
treatment: Immunosuppressants, physical therapy, blood pressure medications
risk_factors: Women 30-50, family history
when_to_see_doctor: If skin hardening or Raynaud's. If difficulty swallowing. If shortness of breath. Early treatment of organ involvement.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Women 30-50, family history
prevention: No proven prevention. Early diagnosis and treatment of complications. Keep warm to manage Raynaud's.

name: Keloid
description: Excessive scar tissue growth beyond wound boundary.
treatment: Corticosteroid injections, cryotherapy, silicone sheets, laser
risk_factors: Darker skin types, family history, young adults
when_to_see_doctor: If growing rapidly. If painful or itchy. If affecting movement or appearance.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Darker skin types, family history, young adults
prevention: Avoid unnecessary skin trauma in prone individuals. Silicone sheets after surgery. Pressure therapy.

name: Intertrigo
description: Inflammatory rash in skin folds.
treatment: Keep skin folds dry, barrier creams, antifungal powders
risk_factors: Infants, obese, diabetics, hot humid climates
when_to_see_doctor: If not improving with basic care. If foul odor or discharge (secondary infection). If diabetic.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Infants, obese, diabetics, hot humid climates
prevention: Keep skin folds clean and dry. Use barrier creams. Absorbent powders. Wear breathable clothing.

name: Miliaria (Heat Rash)
description: Blocked sweat ducts causing tiny bumps in hot conditions.
causes: Obstruction of sweat glands. Most common in hot, humid conditions. Types include miliaria crystalline, rubra (prickly heat), profunda, and pustulosa.
treatment: Cool environment, loose clothing, calamine lotion
risk_factors: Infants, athletes, tropical climates, bedridden patients
when_to_see_doctor: If severe discomfort. If pustules present. If not resolving with cooling measures.
seasonality: Common in infancy but may occur at any age. Typically in hot, humid conditions.
common_in: Infants, athletes, tropical climates, bedridden patients
prevention: Cool environment. Loose, breathable cotton clothing. Cool showers. Avoid heavy creams and ointments.

name: Poison Ivy/Oak/Sumac Dermatitis
description: Allergic contact dermatitis from plant oil.
treatment: Immediate washing, topical/oral corticosteroids, antihistamines
risk_factors: Outdoor workers, hikers, campers, gardeners
when_to_see_doctor: If severe or widespread. If on face or genitals. If not improving with OTC treatments.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Outdoor workers, hikers, campers, gardeners
prevention: Learn to identify plants. Wear protective clothing. Wash skin immediately after exposure. Wash exposed clothing/tools.

name: Nail Fungus (Onychomycosis)
description: Fungal infection of the nail.
treatment: Oral terbinafine, topical antifungal lacquer, laser therapy
risk_factors: Adults, athletes, diabetics, immunocompromised
when_to_see_doctor: If causing pain or difficulty walking. If diabetic (higher complication risk). If OTC treatments fail after 3 months.
seasonality: Not specifically seasonal. Can occur year-round.
common_in: Adults, athletes, diabetics, immunocompromised
prevention: Keep feet clean and dry. Trim nails straight across. Wear breathable shoes. Avoid nail salons with poor hygiene.
